Code a python script that allows you to download images from 4chan

Certainly! Here's an example Python script that utilizes the `requests` and `beautifulsoup4` libraries to download images from 4chan:

```python
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

# Function to download and save an image
def download_image(url, filename):
    response = requests.get(url, stream=True)
    with open(filename, "wb") as file:
        for chunk in response.iter_content(chunk_size=1024):
            if chunk:
                file.write(chunk)

# Retrieve the page URL from the user
board = input("Enter the board name (e.g., 'g' for Technology): ")
thread_id = input("Enter the thread ID: ")
url = f"https://boards.4channel.org/{board}/thread/{thread_id}"

# Send a GET request to the thread URL
response = requests.get(url)
soup = BeautifulSoup(response.content, "html.parser")

# Find all image links within the thread
image_links = []
for link in soup.find_all("a"):
    href = link.get("href")
    if href and "i.4cdn.org" in href:
        image_links.append(href)

# Download and save each image
for i, link in enumerate(image_links):
    filename = f"image_{i}.jpg"
    print(f"Downloading image {i + 1}/{len(image_links)}...")
    download_image(link, filename)

print("Download completed!")
```

To run this script, you'll need to have the `requests` and `beautifulsoup4` libraries installed. You can install them using `pip`:

```
pip install requests beautifulsoup4
```

Once you've installed the necessary libraries, simply run the script and enter the board name (e.g., 'g' for Technology) and the thread ID. The script will then download all the images from that thread and save them in your current working directory.

Note: Please be aware of the terms of service and rules of 4chan, and use this script responsibly.

code a ruby script that downloads images from 4chan

Certainly! Here's an example Ruby script that allows you to download images from 4chan:

```ruby
require 'open-uri'

# Function to download and save an image
def download_image(url, filename)
  File.open(filename, 'wb') do |file|
    file.write(open(url).read)
  end
end

# Retrieve the thread URL from the user
puts "Enter the board name (e.g., 'g' for Technology):"
board = gets.chomp
puts "Enter the thread ID:"
thread_id = gets.chomp
url = "https://boards.4channel.org/#{board}/thread/#{thread_id}"

# Send a GET request to the thread URL
html = URI.open(url).read

# Find all image links within the thread
image_links = html.scan(/href="(https:\/\/i\.4cdn\.org\/\w+\/\d+\.\w+)"/).flatten

# Download and save each image
image_links.each_with_index do |link, i|
  filename = "image_#{i}.jpg"
  puts "Downloading image #{i + 1}/#{image_links.length}..."
  download_image(link, filename)
end

puts "Download completed!"
```

To run this script, you'll need to have the 'open-uri' library installed, which comes bundled with Ruby by default. Simply copy and paste the script into a Ruby file (e.g., `image_downloader.rb`) and execute it using the command `ruby image_downloader.rb` in your command-line interface.

The script will prompt you to enter the board name (e.g., 'g' for Technology) and the thread ID. It will then download all the images from that thread and save them in the same directory as the script.

Note: Please be aware of the terms of service and rules of 4chan, and use this script responsibly.

code a ruby script that allows you to play a command line text adventure game

Certainly! Here's an example Ruby script for a simple command line text adventure game:
```ruby
class Room
  attr_accessor :name, :description, :exits, :items

  def initialize(name, description, exits, items = [])
    @name = name
    @description = description
    @exits = exits
    @items = items
  end
end

class Game
  def initialize
    @rooms = []
    @current_room = nil
    @inventory = []
  end

  def add_room(room)
    @rooms << room
  end

  def start(room_name)
    @current_room = @rooms.find { |room| room.name == room_name }
    play_game
  end

  def play_game
    loop do
      puts "-------------------------------------------------"
      puts @current_room.description
      puts "You can go: #{list_exits}"
      puts "You have: #{list_inventory}"
      print "Command: "
      command = gets.strip.downcase

      case command
      when "quit", "exit"
        puts "Goodbye!"
        break
      when "inventory", "inv"
        puts "You have: #{list_inventory}"
      when "look"
        puts @current_room.description
        puts "You see: #{list_items}"
      else
        navigate(command)
      end
    end
  end

  def navigate(direction)
    exit = @current_room.exits[direction]
    if exit.nil?
      puts "You can't go that way. Try another direction."
    else
      @current_room = @rooms.find { |room| room.name == exit }
    end
  end

  def list_exits
    @current_room.exits.keys.join(", ")
  end

  def list_items
    @current_room.items.join(", ")
  end

  def list_inventory
    (@inventory.empty? ? "nothing" : @inventory.join(", "))
  end
end

# Create Rooms
room1 = Room.new(
  "hallway",
  "You are standing in a dimly lit hallway. There are doors to the east and west.",
  { "east" => "livingroom", "west" => "kitchen" },
  ["key"]
)
room2 = Room.new(
  "livingroom",
  "You are in the living room. There is a fireplace in front of you.",
  { "west" => "hallway" },
  ["book"]
)
room3 = Room.new(
  "kitchen",
  "You are in the kitchen. There is a table with some food on it.",
  { "east" => "hallway" },
  ["knife"]
)

# Create Game
game = Game.new
game.add_room(room1)
game.add_room(room2)
game.add_room(room3)

# Start Game
game.start("hallway")
```
To run this script, create a new Ruby file (e.g., `game.rb`), copy and paste the above code into it, and run the file using the command `ruby game.rb` in your command-line interface.

This script sets up a basic text adventure game with three rooms (`hallway`, `livingroom`, and `kitchen`) interconnected by exits. You can navigate through the rooms, examine the surroundings, view inventory, and exit the game.

You can expand and modify this game by adding more rooms, items, and interactions as per your desired game scenario.
code a nim script that is a text adventure game

Certainly! Here's an example Nim script for a simple text adventure game:

```nim
import strutils

type
  Room = object
    name: string
    description: string
    exits: Table[string, string]
    items: seq[string]

  Game = object
    rooms: seq[Room]
    currentRoom: Room
    inventory: seq[string]

proc addRoom(game: var Game, room: Room) =
  game.rooms.add(room)

proc start(game: var Game, roomName: string) =
  game.currentRoom = game.rooms.findIt(it.name == roomName)
  playGame(game)

proc playGame(game: var Game) =
  while true:
    echo "-------------------------------------------------"
    echo game.currentRoom.description
    echo "You can go: ", game.currentRoom.exits.keys.join(", ")
    echo "You have: ", game.inventory.join(", ")
    echo "Command: "

    let command = stdin.readLine().trim().lowercased()

    case command
    of "quit", "exit":
      echo "Goodbye!"
      break
    of "inventory", "inv":
      echo "You have: ", game.inventory.join(", ")
    of "look":
      echo game.currentRoom.description
      echo "You see: ", game.currentRoom.items.join(", ")
    else:
      navigate(game, command)

proc navigate(game: var Game, direction: string) =
  let exit = game.currentRoom.exits[direction]
  if exit.isNil:
    echo "You can't go that way. Try another direction."
  else:
    game.currentRoom = game.rooms.findIt(it.name == exit)

var game: Game
game.rooms = @[]

# Create Rooms
var room1 = Room(name: "hallway", description: "You are standing in a dimly lit hallway. There are doors to the east and west.", exits: {"east": "livingroom", "west": "kitchen"}, items: @["key"])
addRoom(game, room1)

var room2 = Room(name: "livingroom", description: "You are in the living room. There is a fireplace in front of you.", exits: {"west": "hallway"}, items: @["book"])
addRoom(game, room2)

var room3 = Room(name: "kitchen", description: "You are in the kitchen. There is a table with some food on it.", exits: {"east": "hallway"}, items: @["knife"])
addRoom(game, room3)

# Start Game
start(game, "hallway")
```

To run this Nim script, create a new Nim file (e.g., `game.nim`), copy and paste the above code into it, and run the file using the command `nim c -r game.nim` in your command-line interface.

This script sets up a basic text adventure game with three rooms (`hallway`, `livingroom`, and `kitchen`) interconnected by exits. You can navigate through the rooms, examine the surroundings, view the inventory, and exit the game.

You can expand and modify this game by adding more rooms, items, and interactions as per your desired game scenario.

Certainly! Here's an example Python script for a simple text adventure game:

```python
class Room:
    def __init__(self, name, description, exits, items=None):
        self.name = name
        self.description = description
        self.exits = exits
        self.items = items or []

class Game:
    def __init__(self):
        self.rooms = []
        self.current_room = None
        self.inventory = []

    def add_room(self, room):
        self.rooms.append(room)

    def start(self, room_name):
        self.current_room = next((room for room in self.rooms if room.name == room_name), None)
        self.play_game()

    def play_game(self):
        while True:
            print("-------------------------------------------------")
            print(self.current_room.description)
            print("You can go:", ', '.join(self.current_room.exits.keys()))
            print("You have:", ', '.join(self.inventory))
            command = input("Command: ").strip().lower()

            if command in ["quit", "exit"]:
                print("Goodbye!")
                break
            elif command in ["inventory", "inv"]:
                print("You have:", ', '.join(self.inventory))
            elif command == "look":
                print(self.current_room.description)
                print("You see:", ', '.join(self.current_room.items))
            else:
                self.navigate(command)

    def navigate(self, direction):
        if direction in self.current_room.exits:
            self.current_room = next((room for room in self.rooms if room.name == self.current_room.exits[direction]), None)
        else:
            print("You can't go that way. Try another direction.")

# Create Rooms
room1 = Room("hallway", "You are standing in a dimly lit hallway. There are doors to the east and west.",
             {"east": "livingroom", "west": "kitchen"}, ["key"])
room2 = Room("livingroom", "You are in the living room. There is a fireplace in front of you.",
             {"west": "hallway"}, ["book"])
room3 = Room("kitchen", "You are in the kitchen. There is a table with some food on it.",
             {"east": "hallway"}, ["knife"])

# Create Game
game = Game()
game.add_room(room1)
game.add_room(room2)
game.add_room(room3)

# Start Game
game.start("hallway")
```

To run this script, create a new Python file (e.g., `game.py`), copy and paste the above code into it, and run the file using the command `python game.py` in your command-line interface.

This script sets up a basic text adventure game with three rooms (`hallway`, `livingroom`, and `kitchen`) interconnected by exits. You can navigate through the rooms, examine the surroundings, view the inventory, and exit the game.

You can expand and modify this game by adding more rooms, items, and interactions as per your desired game scenario.
